decision.draft_reply: Hi Daniel — that's a frustrating experience and not what we want for our customers. I've passed this to our Customer Care lead, who'll call you back today to work through it with you. — The Northwind team

rules_applied: SOP Section 2: Customer is unhappy with billing accuracy → COMPLAINT

SOP Section 4: All COMPLAINT messages route to Customer Care
SOP Section 6: Customer threatens online review → flag for human review
Catalogue: Power point installation is $190 fixed per outlet, with surcharge for upper floors noted
judgement_calls: Priority decision between P2 and P3: SOP Section 3 defines P2 as 'any complaint involving a charge over $1,000'. This invoice is $720, below that threshold, which would make it P3. However, the customer threatens a review and the disputed amount ($150) combined with the miscommunication about surcharges suggests escalation risk. The SOP doesn't explicitly define when review threats elevate priority. Going with P2 to ensure 4-hour response window given the review threat, though P3 would be defensible by strict rule application.
reasoning.summary: Customer complains about a $150 billing discrepancy on a power point installation job — quoted $570 but invoiced $720 due to an upper-floor surcharge that wasn't communicated upfront. Customer threatens a review if not resolved. Routing to Customer Care with P2 priority due to review threat, flagged for human review per SOP Section 6.

decision.draft_reply: Hi Marcus — we can definitely service split systems, and the $220 fixed price per unit would apply. Because this is a strata block with 8 units, I've passed it to our Sales team who'll confirm the scope and send through a written quote. They'll be in touch within the day. — The Northwind team

rules_applied: SOP Section 2: Customer asking 'what would the pricing be' → QUOTE category
SOP Section 3: Standard quote request → P3 priority, within 1 business day SLA

Catalogue: 'Anything in commercial premises larger than 200m² — we are residential-only' constraint
SOP Section 6: 'borderline outside our catalogue' → flag for human review
judgement_calls: Catalogue says we don't service commercial premises >200m². This is a residential strata block where each unit is 60-90m² (well under threshold), but the aggregate building is 480-720m² and it's being contracted as a single job by a strata manager. The catalogue constraint is ambiguous on whether 'per premises' means per unit or the whole block. I'm treating each unit as a separate residential premises (which would qualify), but flagging for human review because the aggregate scope and single-contract nature makes this borderline.
reasoning.summary: Quote request for split-system servicing across 8 residential strata units. Each unit qualifies individually under our residential service limit, but the aggregate size and strata-managed nature makes this a borderline case. Routed to Sales with human review flag so they can assess whether to take it on as a bulk job.

decision.draft_reply: Hi Robert — that's not the experience we want for our customers, and I'm sorry your wife was treated that way. Our Customer Care lead will call you back first thing Monday morning to work through this with you properly. — The Northwind team

rules_applied: SOP Section 2: customer is unhappy with tradesperson's conduct → COMPLAINT
SOP Section 4: all COMPLAINT messages route to Customer Care
SOP Section 6: customer mentions tradesperson conduct issue → flag for human review
SOP Section 7: out-of-hours message that is not P1 EMERGENCY is queued for next business morning → acknowledge wait time in draft
judgement_calls: Priority: SOP Section 3 defines P2 as 'any complaint involving a charge over $1,000'. This complaint involves $280 invoice. Default P3 timeline is 1 business day; P2 is 4 business hours. Customer is clearly unhappy but not threatening escalation, no refund requested yet, and the work itself was completed. However, conduct complaints can escalate quickly if not handled promptly. Chose P2 to ensure Monday morning response rather than potentially Tuesday, given this is a conduct issue that reflects on our reputation and the customer was referred by neighbours.
reasoning.summary: Complaint about tradesperson's conduct (muddy boots, short with customer's wife). Work itself was completed and paid ($280 blocked drain). Customer expects conversation about conduct. Flagged for human review per conduct complaint rule. Out-of-hours so queued for Monday morning response. Set P2 to prioritize over standard P3 complaints given reputational sensitivity.
